content.title: Journal
content.intro: A simple stream for dated posts. Use it for notes, essays, updates, or any ongoing body of writing.

content.title: Hello Tetractys
content.excerpt: asdfadsf asdf asdf asd fa la la la la
content.body:
As I'm typing this I have no idea whether what I write will be saved when I click Save. That's what it's like building something new. But also I have to admit, I'm not even sure what the app does.

Content Management sometimes feels like it's in my DNA. When I first began building websites and apps in the 90s, we didn't really have the concept of CMS. We had FrontPage and Dreamweaver, but any time someone wanted to update copy on a live site it tended to come over via email, and then a developer would input it into the site code. Then we'd upload it to a server via FTP and ask the site owner to review and approve it. Sometimes we'd just send screenshots. It was quite an onerous process, and the results were usually not very good.

At some point, someone somewhere got the wise idea to decouple the content from the code and allow a site content editor to safely update text and other embedded media. Overall this was a fantastic idea because it meant that developers could spend their time writing code while the site owners and editors could update content any time they wanted.

For the most part, it worked. There were several CMS platforms on the web throughout the 90s--FileNet, Roxen--and by the late 90s we started to see blogging sites, like LiveJournal and Blogger, come online. In the early 2000s, WordPress was introduced, which now claims 40-60%+ of all CMS-powered websites. Wix, Squarespace, and Shopify are also popular consumer options, with enterprise platforms like Contentstack (where I work as a PM) taking the lead on content personalization and API-first, headless CMS at scale.

It's probably counterintuitive why then I built Tetractys to manage my personal websites, which goes in the complete opposite direction. Tetractys isn't a CMS. It doesn't have RBAC or users. It doesn't have any way to manage media or images. There's no way to extend it. There's really no real reason for anyone but me to use it.

When AI-powered code generation first made the scene, I went back to first principles and started building sites from pure HTML, CSS, PHP and JS. It was wholly unnecessary, as I have ready access to probably the best CMS on the market at work (yes, I'm heavily biased), and anyway I could build and deploy awesome stuff using AI and powering it with NodeJs. The result of that first-principles learning and work is Tectractys.

I found that I could generate any page with any content I wanted via AI, but when I did I lost consistency. Every time I wanted to generate a basic static page, the content and design would change slightly with every iteration. I wanted to simply be able to generate new designs and new words on the page, but I wanted to have control over when things changed.

The way it works is simple: A single JSON file controls all of the content, and it's all flattened into HTML templates when it's deployed. If you're familiar with static site generation, this is that, but so primative as to be laughable. Nobody should use it for anything they are worried about losing. : )

It's getting a little better with every sesh.

content.title: Projects
content.intro: I'm always working on something new and interesting. Below is some of my latest work, from a photo sharing app to surf forecast site and a few things in between.

content.title: Zazen Labs
content.heroText: Zazen Labs is a product studio based in Jacksonville Beach, Florida. We build ventures from the ground up, with deep expertise in strategy, design, web, XR/VR/AR, and AI/ML.
content.body:
Zazen Labs is a product studio that operates at the intersection of focused discipline and modern technology. Based in Jacksonville Beach, Florida, the lab functions as an incubator for internal ventures, specializing in the end-to-end creation of digital products—from initial strategy and design to deployment and long-term maintenance.

Our Philosophy The name "Zazen" refers to seated meditation, a nod to the studio's approach of intentional, centered development. Rather than chasing every trend, the lab focuses on building functional, high-utility products with a deep emphasis on "Vibes," artificial intelligence, and user-centric design.

Active Labs Zazen Labs currently manages a diverse portfolio of active experiments and platforms, including:

PhotoBop: A simplified platform for collective photo sharing among communities and families

Surfy: A highly personalized forecasting tool designed to help surfers align their schedules with their ideal ocean conditions.

Congress Machine: A transparency-focused dashboard that streamlines the process of tracking federal legislation and congressional activity.

Capabilities The studio leverages a specialized technical stack to bring concepts to life, with core expertise in:

Machine Learning & AI: Integrating intelligent automation into everyday tools.

XR/VR/AR: Exploring the boundaries of extended reality and immersive interfaces.

Full-Cycle Development: Handling the rigorous "Ship and Maintain" phase of the product lifecycle.

Zazen Labs is a space where technical expertise in web and mobile architecture meets a methodical, "ground-up" approach to venture building.
content.excerpt: Zazen Labs is a product studio based in Jacksonville Beach, Florida. We build ventures from the ground up, with deep expertise in strategy, design, web, XR/VR/AR, and AI/ML.

content.title: Photo Bop
content.excerpt:
"We searched for a way to collect and share photos that was simple and easy, without the need for accounts, profiles, or downloading apps. We couldn't find it—so we built it."
— Dean & Syd, Co-founders of Photobop
content.body:

content.title: Surfy Life
content.excerpt: A modern surf forecasting platform with a clean visual interface and features that adapt to user preferences
content.body:
Surfy.life is a modern surf forecasting platform designed to provide personalized wave and weather reports for surfers. Unlike traditional forecasting sites that can be cluttered with data, Surfy focuses on a clean, visual-first interface that adapts to individual preferences.

The site uses a proprietary metric called a "Stoke" score (e.g., 4.1 or 5.0). It's an at-a-glance rating that combines wave height, wind speed, and period to tell you how good the conditions are likely to be at a specific break.

Surf Life aggregates data from major meteorological sources—including NOAA, Open Meteo, and the German Weather Service (DWD)—to provide hyper-local conditions.

It highlights popular breaks such as:

Santa Cruz area: Steamer Lane, Pleasure Point, The Hook, and Cowell's.

San Francisco/Half Moon Bay: Ocean Beach and Mavericks.

Central Coast: Morro Rock, Pismo Beach, and Carmel.

Jax Beach Pier: North Side, South Side.

Surfy Life "learns what you like." By signing up, users can set preferences for their favorite spots, and the system provides "intelligent, personalized surf predictions" to help time sessions when conditions align with your specific riding style or skill level.

Instead of traditional passwords, the site uses "Magic Links" sent via email for secure, one-click login. The design is focused on elegant charts and removing the "guesswork" often found in more technical maritime forecasts.

Surfy Life is a streamlined tool for surfers who want a more intuitive, data-driven way to track local swell conditions without wading through complex nautical charts.

content.title: Tetractys
content.heroText: "Probably the coolest thing you have ever built." - Wife
content.excerpt: "Probably the coolest thing you've ever built." - Wife
content.body:
Tetractys is a simple way to deploy static HTML to GitHub Pages. Just click to log in via your GitHub user account, create a new repo, edit the site, and deploy.

The template and content you've updated in the editor are merged, and the site is rendered in GitHub Pages. You can edit the template and schema files in the editor and create new tags on the fly when you need them.

This project is for educational purposes only right now, but you're welcome to try it out at tetractys.zazenlabs.com.

content.title: The Decatur Fist
content.excerpt: The Decatur Fist is my music project, which I started almost 20 years ago in a small town in Texas.

content.title: Congress Machine
content.excerpt: Keeping up with Congress is one of the most important and most difficult parts of being a citizen in the United States. Congress Machine is trying to make that easier.

body.html:
CongressMachine.com is a data-driven legislative tracking dashboard designed to monitor the activities of the United States Congress in real-time. It functions as an aggregator that pulls together various streams of political data into a single interface.

It tracks thousands of pieces of legislation (currently showing over 14,000 bills) across both the House and Senate. You can browse bills by policy area (e.g., Taxation, Health, Environmental Protection) or by specific status.

Congress Machine site highlights newly introduced bills and recently updated legislation, providing summaries and identifying the primary sponsors. It identifies specific representatives (likely based on your location or interests) and provides quick access to their profiles. The platform tracks active members of Congress (over 500) and provides a breakdown of the various committees they serve on.

One of the more distinct features is a "Recent Member Videos" section. It aggregates floor speeches, interviews, and social media clips from members of Congress (e.g., recent clips from Hakeem Jeffries, Bernie Sanders, or John Cornyn) so users can see what representatives are saying publicly.

Congress Machine site includes a section for "Tracked Funding" (currently showing over $868 million), which monitors PAC and party contributions. This allows users to see the financial distribution and contributors behind different political actors.

It is an analytical tool meant for researchers, journalists, or politically engaged citizens to follow the movement of money, legislation, and public messaging within the U.S. federal government.
